coredata.dc:title: Monitoring graphene oxide’s efficiency for removing Re(VII) and Cr(VI) with fluorescent silica hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Chuanqi
Zhang, Yiming
Han, Jiangang
Tian, Ziqi
Chen, Liang
Chen, Jianqiang
coredata.dc:description: Supported carbon quantum dots (CQDs), used as fluorescent sensors for the detection of metal ions, have rarely been used to remove heavy metals from water. Nitrogen-doped CQDs immobilized in hydrophilic silica hydrogels exhibited a more superior sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) than other metal ions, including Fe(III), Fe(II), Zn(II), Cu(II) and Mn(II). For the first time, low limits of detection (LOD) of 2.3 μM for Re(VII) detection and 65 nM for Cr(VI) detection were reported by a facile method. Based on the high selectivity of fluorescent silica hydrogels for Re(VII) and Cr(VI) detection, the removal of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) by graphene oxide (GO) in water was monitored with the hydrogels used as a turn-off fluorescent sensing platform. The consistent results of the sorption isotherms of each metal on GO, which were obtained from the fluorescence spectra and by UV absorption, further verified the possibility of monitoring metal removal by fluorescence detection. Remarkably, GO removed 1186 mg/g of Re(VII) but only 178 mg/g of Cr(VI). The density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicated that both Re(VII) and Cr(VI) formed stable bonds with silica hydrogels, confirming that the interactions between the metal ions and the substrate would promote the fluorescence quenching of the supported CQDs. On the other hand, Re(VII) interacted more strongly with the carboxyl groups of GO than Cr(VI). In addition, a real-time detection system was designed to alarm the service life of a GO filter used for Re(VII) removal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Re(VII) and Cr(VI) detection
Graphene oxide
Silica hydrogels
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of heavy metals and their removal by GO
Characteristics of the N-doped CQDs made from glucose and AMIMCl
Enhanced fluorescence emission of CQDs@silica hydrogels
Monitoring GO’s efficiency for removing Re(VII) and Cr(VI)
Setup of a real-time detection system for alarming the service life of a GO filter

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions
National Natural Science Foundation of China
Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution of the CQDs, (b) XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs, (c) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs, (d) fluorescent spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) AFM images of the silica hydrogels, (b) AFM image of the CQDs@silica hydrogels, (c) TEM image of the CQDs@silica hydrogels, (d) fluorescent spectra (λex. = 340 nm) of the CQD dispersion and CQDs@silica hydrogels.

Fluorescent spectra of the CQD dispersion (a) and the CQDs@silica hydrogels (b) upon the addition of various Re(VII) concentrations, (c) plot of (F 0-F)/F at 425 nm versus the Re(VII) concentration, (d) fluorescent images obtained with identical CQDs@silica hydrogels with various Re(VII) concentrations under a UV light.

(a) Fluorescent intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels obtained with different incubation times and 6.0 × 10−4 M metal ion concentrations, (b) the enhanced quenching efficiency (F 0/F at 425 nm) from the CQD dispersion to the CQDs@silica hydrogels with various heavy metals, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) (black line) and the fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the CQDs, (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of Re(VII) (black line) and the fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescent spectra of the CQDs@silica hydrogels, which was used to indicate GO’s efficiency for removing Re(VII) (a) and Cr(VI) (b), with 1.0 g of GO, and the correlation of the metal concentration in the pre- (C 0) and post-treated water(C t) (c and d).
Langmuir model isotherms of Re(VII) (a) and Cr(VI) (b) sorption on graphene at a temperature of 298 K.
Schematic diagram of the real-time detection system used for alarming the service life of a GO filter.
Optimized geometries of the silica hydrogels and the interactions of the silica hydrogels with ReO4− and CrO4 2−.

head.ce:title: Monitoring graphene oxide’s efficiency for removing Re(VII) and Cr(VI) with fluorescent silica hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, College of Biology and the Environment, Nanjing Forestry University, 159 Longpan Road, Nanjing, 210037, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biology and the Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, College of Biology and the Environment, Nanjing Forestry University, 159 Longpan Road, Nanjing 210037, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo, 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo 315201, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contribute equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supported carbon quantum dots (CQDs), used as fluorescent sensors for the detection of metal ions, have rarely been used to remove heavy metals from water. Nitrogen-doped CQDs immobilized in hydrophilic silica hydrogels exhibited a more superior sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) than other metal ions, including Fe(III), Fe(II), Zn(II), Cu(II) and Mn(II). For the first time, low limits of detection (LOD) of 2.3 μM for Re(VII) detection and 65 nM for Cr(VI) detection were reported by a facile method. Based on the high selectivity of fluorescent silica hydrogels for Re(VII) and Cr(VI) detection, the removal of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) by graphene oxide (GO) in water was monitored with the hydrogels used as a turn-off fluorescent sensing platform. The consistent results of the sorption isotherms of each metal on GO, which were obtained from the fluorescence spectra and by UV absorption, further verified the possibility of monitoring metal removal by fluorescence detection. Remarkably, GO removed 1186 mg/g of Re(VII) but only 178 mg/g of Cr(VI). The density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicated that both Re(VII) and Cr(VI) formed stable bonds with silica hydrogels, confirming that the interactions between the metal ions and the substrate would promote the fluorescence quenching of the supported CQDs. On the other hand, Re(VII) interacted more strongly with the carboxyl groups of GO than Cr(VI). In addition, a real-time detection system was designed to alarm the service life of a GO filter used for Re(VII) removal.
Re(VII) and Cr(VI) absorption by GO was monitored by the fluorescence quenching of CQDs@silica hydrogels with a high sensitivity for metal detection.

ce:section.ce:para: In the nuclear industry, radioactive technetium-99 (99Tc) is concomitantly generated as a fission product of 235U or 239Pu at relatively high yields of approximately 6.1% (Li et al., 2017). As the most stable oxidation state of 99Tc, the pertechnetate anion (TcO4 −) is highly soluble in water and thus poses great environmental risks to human health (Katayev et al., 2009). ReO4 − (Re(VII)) was often used as a non-radioactive surrogate to mimic the anionic behaviours of radioactive TcO4 −, owing to ReO4 − and TcO4 − having similar electronic distributions, spatial configurations, and thermodynamic parameters (Pierce et al., 2017). The determination and quantification of rhenium was mostly applied in the mining industry and botanic research by spectrophotometric (Bozhkov and Borisova, 2002), spectral (Metody et al., 2016; Li et al., 2010), and voltammetric methods (Kolpakova and Golts, 2007), all of which required large and expensive instruments, complicated operation procedures and specially trained personnel.

Over the past decades, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with unique optical and physiochemical properties have been intensively investigated for use as sensor probes to detect a variety of heavy metals, including Hg(II), Cu(II), Ag(I), Zn(II), Cr(VI), Fe(III), As(III), Cd(II), Co(II), Pb(II), etc., in water (Lim et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2016; Devi et al., 2019; He et al., 2016). Among these heavy metals, the detection of Cr(VI) in water is of great importance due to its high toxicity (Gomez and Callao, 2006). Absorption of high Cr(VI) levels in vivo can lead to dermatitis, stomach ulcers and different types of cancer (Barrera-Díaz et al., 2012; Qian et al., 2017). The fundamental mechanism for Cr(VI) detection with CQDs was based on the Cr(VI)-induced fluorescence quenching of the CQDs in response to the interactions between Cr(VI) and the CQDs (Devi et al., 2019). The sensitivity and selectivity of CQD-based fluorescent probes for Cr(VI) detection was greatly improved through the modification of CQDs by introducing dopants (Cai et al., 2014), surface passivation (Pooja et al., 2019) and surface functionalization (Liu et al., 2018). However, there remained a major challenge for the commercialization of dispersed CQDs for metals detection (Devi et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2018). Remarkably, substrate-assisted CQDs have been able to play an important role in the precise quantification of heavy metals. Graphene quantum dots immobilized on various types of membranes (nylon, cellulose and glass fibre) exhibited a wide Cr(VI) detection window of 1–500 mM and a low detection limit of 190 nM, in addition to an enhanced sensitivity and selectivity (Carrasco et al., 2016). CQDs can be supported on solid or soft substrates, e.g., glass (Wang et al., 2016), paper (Yuan et al., 2014; Gupta et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2017), PVA films and stainless steel (Han et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2014), to conduct heavy metal monitoring. CQDs with a low quantum yield (QY) exhibited a high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of heavy metals after their immobilization onto the substrates. Existing supported CQD systems mostly focused on the active aggregation of CQDs on the substrate. However, the hypothesis that the binding of metal ions to the substrate could promote the fluorescence quenching of supported CQDs, thus enhancing the sensitivity and LOD of the metal detection method, has not been verified. For practical applications, the use of supported CQDs for detecting metal ions in water has not been substantially applied practically, for example, to indicate the service life of metal adsorbents.

Silica aerogels have the advantages of a large surface area, an extremely low density, a low thermal conductivity, a weak dielectric permittivity, etc., and are widely used as insulation materials (Wei et al., 2011), adsorbent and filter materials (Han et al., 2016; Mi et al., 2018), electrodes and catalyst supports (Dundar et al., 2012). Remarkably, due to the high transparency of silica aerogels, silica aerogels supported CQDs have great potential for acting as on-off-type fluorescent sensors. Wang et al. (2013) prepared CQD-aerogel hybrid materials by immobilizing CQDs onto silica aerogels for detecting NO2 in the atmosphere. Until now, there exist several studies on the synthesis and fluorescent properties of CQD-silica matrices (Dolai et al., 2017; Brito et al., 2015; Fu et al., 2015). To the best of our knowledge, however, no work has been conducted on hydrophilic CQDs@silica hydrogels used for the detection of heavy metals in water.

In the present study, as shown in Scheme S1, we first prepared hydrophilic N-doped CQDs@silica hydrogels for the detection of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) in addition to other metal ions, including Fe(III), Fe(II), Zn(II), Cu(II) and Mn(II). To monitor the removal of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) by a GO filter, fluorescent silica hydrogels were used as fluorescent sensors to indicate GO’s efficiency to remove both metal ions. The results revealed that the fluorescent silica hydrogels possessed a more superior sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) compared to the dispersed CQDs and GO, which exhibited a remarkable capability for removing Re(VII). At this point, the molecular interactions of metal ions with silica hydrogels and GO were investigated by density functional theory calculations to provide a theoretical explanation. Moreover, an alarm system for monitoring real-time Re(VII) removal was designed.

Glucose anhydrous (>95%), 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AMIMCl, >99%), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, ≥98%), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7%), quinine sulfate (95%) and ammonium perrhenate (NH4ReO4) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China. Hydrochloric acid (30 wt%), aqueous ammonia (28–30 wt%), ZnCl2, MnSO4, CuSO4, FeSO4, FeCl3, CrO3, K2HPO4 and KH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from the Tan Yuan Graphene (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used for all experiments.

First, 3.0 g of glucose anhydrous was mixed with 9.0 g of AMIMCl in a three-necked flask under vigorous stirring for 12 h. The viscous mixture was carbonized under a nitrogen atmosphere in a tube furnace (GSL-1700X, Hefei Kejing Materials & Technology Co., Ltd., China) by heating to 250 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, holding at 250 °C for 2 h, and then cooling to room temperature. Then, 2.0 g of the yielded products was dispersed in 200 mL of water under stirring, which was followed by 1.0 h of ultrasonication. The resultant dispersion was centrifuged to remove the precipitate, and the yellowish CQD solution was collected.

The silica aerogels were first synthesized by a sol-gel method, which was followed by a supercritical drying process. TEOS was used as the silicon precursors, EtOH and deionized water were used as solvents, and hydrochloride and aqueous ammonia were used as catalysts. Next, 5 mL of TEOS and 15 mL of EtOH were stirred well, and then, 5 μL of hydrochloride and 5 mL of distilled water were added to the solution for the hydrolysis of TEOS. The gelation of silicic acid was triggered by adding 15 μL of aqueous ammonia. In the drying process, the wet silica gels were placed in an autoclave, with CO2 as the dry medium, and EtOH was added into the autoclave until the gels were submerged. After that, the wet silica gels were directly dried with EtOH in a supercritical fluid generator. The immobilization of the CQDs onto the silica hydrogels was realized by an incubation method. Under stirring, 2 mL of the CQD solution (10 mg/mL) was quantitatively added to 0.15 g pf the silica aerogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy metals and their removal by GO
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching of the CQDs@silica hydrogels as a function of the metal concentration was investigated by using the hydrogels as turn-off fluorescent sensors and compared with that of the dispersed CQDs in solution. The metal concentration in each of the water samples was set in the range of 0–1.6 × 10−3 M. For each detection scheme, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) as follows:
(1)
L O D =
3 σ
K
s v
(2)
F 0
− F
F
=
K
s v
×
C metal
+ B
where σ is the standard deviation of the blank response, K SV is the slope of the calibration curve, F and F 0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the CQDs in solution and CQDs@silica hydrogels at 420 nm in the presence and absence of heavy metals, respectively, C metal is the concentration of the heavy metals, and B represents the intercept of the calibration curve. For the removal of the heavy metals by GO, 1.0 g of GO was added as an adsorbent to each water sample under stirring for 2 min. The GO and water sample as a whole was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm, and then, the supernatant was obtained for characterization.
The isotherms of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) absorption by GO were studied using Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption models (Zhao et al., 2011), respectively.

Langmuir adsorption isotherm:
(3)
q =
q
max
×
K
L
×
C
eq
1 +
K
L
×
C
eq

Freundlich adsorption isotherm:
(4)
q =
K F
×
C
1 n
eq

C
eq (mg/L) is the final concentration of the metal ions in the aqueous solutions after sorption equilibration, q is the amount of metal ions adsorbed on GO (mg/g), q max is the maximum amount of metal ions adsorbed on GO to completely cover the surfaces, K L represents the enthalpy of sorption, and K F and n are the Freundlich constants related to the sorption capacity and sorption intensity, respectively.

The study of adsorption kinetics is helpful for better understanding the adsorption process. Commonly used adsorption kinetics fitting models are pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order fitting models. The fitting equation is as follows:

Traditional dynamics:
dq
t
d t
=
k
n

−

)
n

Pseudo-first-order (n = 1):
ln

)
= ln

1
t

Pseudo-second-order (n = 2):
t
q
t
=
1

2

2
+
t
q
e

In the abovementioned equations, q
e is the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g), q
t is the adsorption amount corresponding to the reaction time t (mg/g), and n is the kinetics reaction rate constant.

The morphologies of the CQDs and GO were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-1400, JEOL, Japan). The CQDs and GO dispersed in ethanol were dipped on carbon-coated copper grids, and then, the ethanol was evaporated under an infrared lamp. An accelerating voltage of 200 kV and the bright-field image mode were set. The morphologies of the silica aerogels and CQDs@silica hydrogels were characterized using an atomic force microscope (Dimension Edge, Bruker Nano Surfaces, USA) equipped with a Nanosensors tip/cantilever. The FT-IR spectra of the CODs and GO were recorded for a wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 by a spectrophotometer (VERTEX-80V, Bruker, Germany) with a 4 cm−1 resolution. The Raman spectra were recorded by Raman spectroscopy (DXR532, Themo, USA), using an excitation wavelength of 633 nm. The UV absorption and fluorescent spectra were recorded on a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (V-570, Jasco, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS55, PE, USA), respectively. The XPS measurements were performed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AXIS UltraDLD, Shimadzu, Japan). The BET test of the silica aerogels was conducted with an ASAP2020M instrument (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA).

Gaussian 09 was applied to conduct the density functional theory (DFT) method to explore the molecular interactions of the heavy metals with GO and the silica hydrogels. GaussView 5.0 was used for molecular visualization. All the binding configurations and energies of the accurate ground state geometries were obtained by using DFT. The adsorption energy (E ad) was calculated by Eq. (5):
(5)
E
ad
=
E
1
+
E
2
−
E
12
where E 1 is the energy of the silica hydrogels and GO, E 2 is the energy of the heavy metals, and the E 12 represents the energy of the metals@silica hydrogels and metals@GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the N-doped CQDs made from glucose and AMIMCl
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were produced by calcining the mixture of glucose and AMIMCl at 250 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere, where the surfaces of the CQDs were decorated with ionic liquids, and accordingly, optical and catalytic functions were applied (Pham-Truong et al., 2018). The TEM image (Fig. 1 (a)) shows that the CQDs possessed spherical shapes and a monodisperse size distribution in the range of 2–7 nm with an average size at 4.13 nm. From the HRTEM image shown in the inset, the CQD exhibited an apparently parallel arranged layered structure with an interlay spacing of 0.253 nm. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 1(b)) demonstrate the surface elemental composition of the carbon materials prepared with and without AMIMCl. The peak at approximately 402 eV, which was associated with N 1s, was observed in the spectrum of the CQDs prepared with AMIMCl. In the Supplementary Materials, the deconvolution of the C1s and N1s (Fig. S1) peaks indicated the nitrogen doping of the CQDs, which was attributed to the incorporation of the imidazolium rings of AMIMCl into the bulky carbon during the pyrolysis of glucose (Paraknowitsch et al., 2010). In the Raman spectrum (Fig. S2), the sharp strong peaks at 1580 cm−1 correspond to the G band and indicate the sp2 hybridization of the CQDs, while the peak centred at 1350 cm−1 corresponds to the D band and indicates the presence of an sp3 carbon structure (Qiao et al., 2018). The intensity ratio of the two peaks (I D/I G) denoting the degree of disorder was calculated to be 0.82, implying that the highly defective structure in the CQDs was possibly caused by the introduction of edge functional groups into the CQDs (Li et al., 2012). As shown in the XRD patterns of the CQD powders (Fig. S3), the broad peak centred at 23.26° along with the minor peak centred at 12.25° agreed well with the typical patterns of graphene, which has an amorphous structure (Zhu et al., 2009). As shown in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1(c)), the strong peak at 3390 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the O–H groups. The peaks at 3016 and 1569 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching and bending vibrations of N–H, while the peaks at 1454 and 1680 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C–N and CO, respectively, and indicated the presence of the amide groups of the CQDs. All of these groups indicated that the CQD surfaces were hydrophilic.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. S4) exhibits two absorption bands centred at 325 and 380 nm, which were associated with the n→π∗ transition of CO (Zhang et al., 2012). The fluorescence emission of the CQDs (Fig. 1(d)) exhibited excitation-dependent characteristics and emission peaks ranging from 400 to 575 nm under excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 to 400 nm. The most intense fluorescence emission was observed at 425 nm and was achieved with an excitation wavelength at 340 nm. Under UV irradiation at 360 nm, the yellowish CQD solution emitted an intense blue light. The quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was approximately 9.84% (Table S1), which was higher than that of the bare CQDs prepared from glucose in previous studies (Bourlinos et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2009). The fluorescence intensity rarely decayed under continuous excitation for 12 h (Fig. S5), which indicated that the CQDs had an excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced fluorescence emission of CQDs@silica hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the AFM image (Fig. 2 (a)), the morphologies of the silica hydrogels resembled a forest landscape, which reflected a sponge-like structure with abundant nanopores. Based on the BET test, the dry silica aerogels had a large specific area of 803.63 m2/g and an average pore size of 15.1 nm, which was assumed to facilitate an effective loading of the CQDs (Fig. S6). After incorporation of the CQDs (Fig. 2(b)), the surface of the silica hydrogels became smooth, and the roughness decreased from 9.38 to 6.08 because a certain number of CQDs was immobilized onto the hydrogels to fill the nanopores. The TEM image (Fig. 2(c)) further demonstrates the morphology of the silica hydrogels after the incorporation of the CQDs. It is apparent that the silica hydrogels exhibited an interconnected porous structure, with a large number of spherical CQDs uniformly distributed in the networks, as indicated by the white arrows and circles. The immobilization of the CQDs was possibly achieved through hydrogen bonds forming between the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the CQDs and silicon-oxygen networks of the silica hydrogels (Wang et al., 2013; Ma et al., 2019).

Interestingly, in comparison to that of CQDs dispersed in water, the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs@silica hydrogels (Fig. 2(d)) exhibited not only a more intense fluorescence emission under the same excitation wavelength but also a maximum emission that was 15 nm blueshifted, which suggested the enhanced fluorescent property of the CQDs after their immobilization onto the hydrogels (Carrasco et al., 2016). Under a UV lamp, the CQDs@silica hydrogels in the beaker emitted a strong blue light with uniform brightness.

The fluorescence spectra of the CQD dispersion and CQDs@silica hydrogels in the presence of concentrations Re(VII) ranging from 0 to 1.6 × 10−3 M are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively. The fluorescence intensity was reduced by only 6.88% after adding 2.0 × 10−4 M Re(VII), reflecting the slow fluorescence quenching of the CQD dispersion. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was quenched by 25.10% with the same metal concentration. Such a difference was attributed to the CQDs immobilized on the silica hydrogels, which were more likely to approach Re(VII) compared with the same amount of CQDs dispersed in solution. The fluorescence curves of the dispersed CQDs overlapped one another, despite the increasing Re(VII) concentrations (Fig. 3(a)), indicating that the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs in solution was not very sensitive to Re(VII) concentrations. To study the factors influencing the sensing system, we performed temperature and pH dependent experiments, and the results are shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, respectively. The ordinate of the curves represents the quenching of the fluorescence emission ((F 0-F)/F, where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities after and before metal addition). The higher the value, the higher the sensitivity of the quantum dots for detecting metals. As shown in Fig. S7, for both Re(VII) and Cr(VI) (with initial concentrations of 6.0 × 10−4 M and 2.0 × 10−4 M, respectively), the sensitivity reached the highest value at 25 °C. As the temperature further increased, the sensitivity gradually decreased. The enhanced sensitivity may be attributed to the accelerated molecular motion, which is due to the increase in the temperature. On the other hand, the collisions between the CQDs also probably increased, which led to non-radiative transitions and thereby reduced the fluorescence efficiency of the CQDs (Faustino et al., 2013).

In terms of pH effects, Fig. S8 shows that the CQDs have a high sensitivity for Re(VII) detection at pH values of 6.0–9.0 and a high sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection at pH values of 5.0–8.0. Based on the FT-IR spectrum of the N-doped CQDs (Fig. 1(c)), it can be inferred that the CQD surface is rich in nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups, such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups. The solution pH affected the degree of deprotonation and the speciation of the surface functional groups. With increasing the pH, the surface charge is more negative, and the electrostatic interactions between the metal ions and the CQDs become stronger, thereby increasing metal ion sorption on the carbon surface (Zhao et al., 2011).

In the meantime, the CQDs@silica hydrogels exhibited distinct fluorescence quenching properties with the same increase in the Re(VII) concentrations (Fig. 3(b)), which implied that Re(VII) was much more inclined to trigger the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs@silica hydrogels than that of the CQDs dispersed in water at the same ion concentrations. Fig. 3(c) shows the linear correlation of the fluorescence intensity of the CQD dispersion and CQDs@silica hydrogels with the Re(VII) concentrations, as obtained by fitting Eq. (2). An apparent linear correlation was observed between the values of (F 0-F)/F and Re(VII) concentrations in the range of 0–1.6 × 10−3 M for both systems. Based on Eq. (2), the Stern-Volmer constant (K SV) was calculated to be 1264.8 M-1 for the CQDs@silica hydrogels and 113.2 M-1 for the CQD dispersion. It is well known that the Stern-Volmer constant reflected either dynamic or collisional quenching (Athina et al., 2005). The large Stern-Volmer constant indicated that the CQDs@silica hydrogels were highly sensitive when used as a fluorescence turn-off sensing system. In addition, the LODs of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was 2.3 μM, whereas that of the CQD dispersion was up to 11.4 μM. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the brightness of the CQDs@silica hydrogels irradiated by a UV lamp visibly faded with increasing Re(VII) concentrations, indicating the feasibility of their application as a visual detection device.

The CQD dispersion and CQDs@silica hydrogels exhibited a similar trend in terms of their fluorescence quenching with Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 4.0 × 10−5 M to 1.4 × 10−3 M (Figs. S9(a) and S9(b)). The fluorescent intensity of the CQD dispersion was quenched by 51.22% by adding 2.0 × 10−4 M Cr(VI), and that value was increased to 63.03% for the CQDs@silica hydrogels, indicating that the CQDs in both systems were very sensitive to the presence of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. S9(c), the fitted K SV value of 7290.1 M-1 obtained for the CQDs@silica hydrogels suggested a high sensitivity for detecting Cr(VI). The slope of the linear correlation obtained for the CQDs@silica hydrogels was obviously larger than that obtained for the CQD dispersion. As a result, the visible change in the brightness of the CQDs@silica hydrogels with an increase in the Cr(VI) concentration is shown in Fig. S9(d) . The LODs of 65 nM obtained by the CQDs@silica hydrogels was far below the maximum level of 1 μM allowed in drinking water by the World Health Organization (WHO Press, 2011).

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), with the addition of 6.0 × 10−4 M metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was quenched to a low level within several seconds for both metals, which indicated that the CQDs@silica hydrogels rapidly interacted with the metal ions, giving rise to the fluorescence quenching. Notably, when the incubation time exceeded 18 min, the remaining fluorescence response of the CQDs@silica hydrogels kept constant without further decay. Various metal ions, including Zn(II), Mn(II), Cu(II), Fe(II) and Fe(III), were detected by the CQD dispersion and CQDs@silica hydrogels, as shown in Fig. S10∼S14 in the Supplementary Materials. As summarized in Fig. 4(b), we chose a fixed concentration of 0.6 mM for all the ions. The quenching efficiency (F 0/F) of the CQD dispersion was subtracted from that of the CQDs@silica hydrogels. The obviously enhanced F 0/F value of the CQDs@silica hydrogels further proved that the selectivity for Cr(VI) and Re(VII) detection was higher than that for the other five metal ions.

To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the CQDs for Cr(VI) and Re(VII) detection, the combined fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) spectra of the CQDs and UV–vis absorbance spectra of Cr(VI) and Re(VII) were investigated (Fig. 4(c) and (d)). As shown in Fig. 4(c), Cr(VI) showed a strong absorption peak at 351 nm, while the CQDs@silica hydrogels exhibited maximum excitation and emission peaks at 340 nm and 420 nm, respectively. Therefore, the absorption of Cr(VI) was substantially overlapped by the excitation and emission of CQDs@silica, leading to the emission of the CQDs being partly absorbed and shielded by Cr(VI). Thus, the inner filter effect (IFE) theory used to illustrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-doped carbon dots induced by Cr(VI) can be accordingly applied to this study (Zhang et al., 2018) to show that the CQDs prepared in this study were able to selectively detect Cr(VI). The peak corresponding to Re(VII) ion absorption was located at 265 nm and partly overlapped the excitation peak of the CQDs (Fig. 4(d)). The blueshifting of the emission peak after the incorporation of the CQDs into silica hydrogels made the emission peak closer to the Re(VII) adsorption peak, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs@silica hydrogels by Re(VII) was more pronounced than that of the CQD dispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring GO’s efficiency for removing Re(VII) and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. 5 (a) and (b) that when the Re(VII) concentration was set at 2.0 × 10−4 M, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was recovered to 96.8% after the Re(VII)-containing water was treated with 1.0 g of GO, indicating that the majority of the Re(VII) ions in the water were removed by GO (Fig. 5(a)). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity recovered only to 20.8% after the removal of Cr(VI), which indicated that the sorption of Cr(VI) by GO was not as effective as that of Re(VII) under the same condition.

In the case of higher metal concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels can be recovered to 91.2% and 88.6% after the sorption of Re(VII) concentrations of 4.0 × 10−4 M and 6.0 × 10−4 M by GO, respectively, which indicated that under a high metal concentration, GO was still highly efficient at removing high Re(VII) concentrations. However, in the Cr(VI)-containing water, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels recovered only to 48.6% for Cr(VI) concentrations of 4.0 × 10−4 M, and the recovery decreased to 31.3% for Cr(VI) concentrations of 6.0 × 10−4 M, reflecting GO’s inferior performance for removing high Cr(VI) concentrations. The concentration of a series of metals in the raw water (C 0) can be reduced to a certain concentration (C t) after the treatment with 1.0 g of GO and can be calculated based on the linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the metal concentrations, as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S9. Consequently, the correlation between C 0 and C t can be fitted as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d) for Re(VII) and Cr(VI), respectively. By stoichiometric sorption, 1.0 g of GO was supposed to be able to remove all the metal ions in the water when C 0 exceeded the critical concentration, below which the complete removal of the metal ions can be achieved. Based on the linear correlation between C 0 and C t, the maximum concentration of Cr(VI) in the inflow water can be estimated to be 0.102 mM according to the concentration allowed in effluent water (6.7 μM) by the Chinese national standard (GB18466-2005). Likewise, a maximum Re(VII) concentration of 0.146 mM can be obtained in the inflow water.

Fig. 6 shows the isotherms of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) adsorption by GO at 298 K based on Eqs. (3) and (4). We used fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption to verify the adsorption isotherm equilibrium of the two metals. The sorption curves obtained by the two methods were simulated with the Langmuir model. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the Langmuir isothermal curves obtained for Re(VII) adsorption by the two methods reflected a similar trend, with an R2 value greater than 0.99, which proved the feasibility of using the fluorescence method to determine the adsorption of Re(VII) by GO in water. The curves fitted by the Freundlich model are shown in Fig. S15(a). The relative parameters calculated from the two abovementioned models are summarized in Table S2. Remarkably, the q max value of 1186 mg/g was obtained by the fluorescence method for the indication of Re(VII) adsorption by GO, which agrees well with the result of 1194 mg/g obtained by UV adsorption. These results provided direct evidence that GO was among the adsorbents capable of effectively removing radioactive elements (Williams and Carbone, 2016). With respect to the adsorption of Cr(VI) by GO, the comparable results obtained by the fluorescence method and UV–visible absorption, as shown in Fig. 6(b), Fig. S15(b) and Table S2 , also verified the good accuracy achieved by the fluorescence method. From the R2 values, the Langmuir model seemed to fit the sorption isotherms better than the Freundlich model for each metal, revealing the monolayer sorption of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) by GO.

As shown in Fig. S16, adsorption is divided into two stages: the adsorption rate is fast in the first 90 min because of the large metal concentration gradient between the surface of the adsorbent and the solution. As a result, metal ions can quickly bind to the active sites on the surface of the adsorbent (Senthil Kumar et al., 2010). After 90 min, adsorption gradually reached saturation. The fitting results are shown in Table S3. The correlation coefficient R2 of the pseudo-second-order kinetics equation is much larger than that of the pseudo-first-order kinetics equation. Therefore, the adsorption of metals by GO is fitted better by the pseudo-second-order kinetics equations than the pseudo-first order equation.

The sorption of a high Cr(VI) concentration of 6.0 × 10−4 M with different amounts of GO was studied, as shown in Fig. S17. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was recovered only to 54.94% and 77.15% when 10.0 g and 20.0 g of GO was applied, respectively. These findings indicated that the quantity of GO played a critical role in the removal of Cr(VI) due to its inferior ability to adsorb high Cr(VI) concentrations.

As shown in Table 1 , a fragment of polysilicic acid (Si–OH) was selected as the model to investigate the molecular interactions of the silica hydrogels with ReO4 − and CrO4 2−. With GaussView 5.0, the optimized geometries of ReO4 − and CrO4 2− binding to the silica hydrogels were obtained. It can be seen from the optimized geometries of both ReO4 − and CrO4 2− that the anions could form bonds with the H atoms of Si–OH with bond lengths less than 2 Å. The binding of ReO4 − and CrO4 2− to the silica hydrogels could possibly accelerate the fluorescence quenching of the immobilized CQDs. Therefore, Table 1 verified the aforementioned hypothesis that the interactions between the metal ions and the substrate would promote the fluorescence quenching of the supported CQDs, thus enhancing the sensitivity and LOD of the metal detection method. Interestingly, CrO4 2− formed a shorter bond length (1.781 Å) with Si–OH than ReO4 − (1.870 Å), implying that CrO4 2− formed more stable bonds than ReO4 − with the silica hydrogels, which agrees well with the results indicating that the system is more sensitive and has a lower LOD for detecting Cr(VI) than Re(VII).

The FT-IR spectrum of GO (Fig. S18) showed characteristic peaks at 1648 cm−1, 1060 cm−1 and 3410 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CO, C–O and -OH group, indicating the presence of -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of GO nanosheets with limited thicknesses, as shown in the TEM image (Fig. S19). Accordingly, GO(-OH) and GO(-COOH), which represent the edge of the GO nanosheets, were selected as models of the metal adsorbents. The optimized geometries of GO(-COOH) for the adsorption of ReO4 − and CrO4 2− are listed in Table 2 . It is obvious that the bond length (1.634 Å) between GO(-COOH) and ReO4 − was much shorter than that between GO(-COOH) and CrO4 2− (2.198 Å), indicating the superior performance of the GO nanosheets for absorbing ReO4 −. Moreover, the adsorption energy (E ad, 180.60 kcal/mol) of GO(-COOH) for the sorption of ReO4 − was higher than that for the sorption of CrO4 2− (114.33 kcal/mol), which also indicated that ReO4 − was more inclined than CrO4 2− to be adsorbed by GO. Similarly, as shown in Table S4, the much shorter bond length and higher E ad of GO(–OH)/ReO4 − indicated that ReO4 − was more efficiently adsorbed by GO(–OH) than CrO4 2−. By comparing the data shown in Table 2 and Table S4, we can easily identify that the -COOH groups on the GO nanosheets were more efficient at adsorbing Re(VII) than the -OH groups, indicating that the carboxyl groups on the GO surface played a more favourable role in the removal of ReO4 −, which agreed well with the conclusion of a previously reported study (Wang et al., 2018). Furthermore, the results of Table 2 and Table S4 provided theoretical evidence of the results shown in Fig. 5(a) and (c), showing that after the efficient adsorption of Re(VII) by the GO nanosheets, the removal of Re(VII) can be indicated through the distinct fluorescence recovery of the CQDs@silica hydrogels. The conclusions drawn from Table 2 and Table S4 also agreed well with the results of the adsorption isotherm shown in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Setup of a real-time detection system for alarming the service life of a GO filter
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , the alarm system is mainly composed of a GO filter and a detection module, where the CQDs@silica hydrogels loaded in a glass capillary, a UV light source, a photoelectric convertor and a display screen were integrated. A sufficient amount of GO was loaded in the filter so that the metal ions in the inflowing water could be completely removed. In the detection module, an excitation wavelength of 340 nm was obtained from the UV source. The fluorescence emission of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was detected by the photoelectric convertor, and the fluorescent signals can be effectively converted into electrical signals. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@silica hydrogels was stable when the GO filter worked normally. However, when the GO filter reached the service life, the rapid fluorescence quenching of the CQDs@silica hydrogels indicated that the metal ions began to penetrate through the GO filter, which would trigger the cut-off of the water supply. In this case, the CQDs dispersed in solution cannot act as a real-time detection probe, because the immobilized CQDs would be swept off by the water flow. Practically, this detection system can be applied for family-use drinking water filters, in which the users never know the actual service life of the adsorbent, and only the fixed service time is printed in the product manual.

In conclusion, the fluorescent silica hydrogels synthesized in this work exhibited a superior sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) ranging from 0 to 1.6 × 10−3 M with low LODs of 2.3 μM and 65 nM. The fluorescent silica hydrogels can be used as a turn-off fluorescent sensing platform for effectively indicating the absorption of Re(VII) and Cr(VI) by GO in water. Moreover, DFT calculations revealed that the binding of metal ions to silica substrates promoted the fluorescence quenching of supported CQDs, thus enhancing the sensitivity and LOD of the metal detection. This study provided a real-time detection system for alarming the service life of a GO filter used for Re(VII) removal from nuclear wastewater.
Ziqi Tian: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Liang Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jianqiang Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51603104), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20160921), and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province.
